questions.question: Explain the difference between time complexity and space complexity with examples.
keyPoints: Time complexity measures execution time as function of input size (e.g., O(n), O(log n))
Space complexity measures memory usage during execution
Time: how long algorithm takes; Space: how much memory it needs
Example: MergeSort has O(n log n) time and O(n) space complexity

questions.question: Describe the Divide and Conquer algorithm design technique with an example.
keyPoints: Divide: Break problem into smaller subproblems
Conquer: Solve subproblems recursively
Combine: Merge solutions of subproblems
Example: MergeSort, QuickSort, Binary Search use this approach
Reduces complex problem into manageable parts

questions.question: What is recursion? Explain with an example and mention its base case.
keyPoints: Recursion is a function calling itself
Base case is the terminating condition preventing infinite loops
Example: Factorial calculation: n! = n × (n-1)!
Without base case, infinite recursion occurs
Stack overflow happens when recursion depth exceeds limit

questions.question: Compare Linear Search and Binary Search algorithms.
keyPoints: Linear search: O(n) time, works on unsorted arrays, checks each element
Binary search: O(log n) time, requires sorted array, divides search space in half
Binary search is faster but needs pre-sorted data
Linear search is simple but inefficient for large datasets
Implementation: iterative or recursive for binary search

questions.question: Explain the working of Stack and Queue data structures with real-life examples.
keyPoints: Stack: LIFO principle - like a stack of plates or browser back button
Queue: FIFO principle - like people standing in ticket line
Stack operations: push, pop, peek
Queue operations: enqueue, dequeue, front, rear
Both are linear data structures with restricted access

questions.question: What is Big O notation? Explain its significance in analyzing algorithms.
keyPoints: Big O describes upper bound of algorithm complexity
Used to express worst-case performance
Common complexities: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²)
Helps compare algorithms independent of hardware
Ignores constants, focuses on growth rate

questions.question: Describe the tree traversal methods with their order of visiting nodes.
keyPoints: Preorder: Root → Left → Right
Inorder: Left → Root → Right (gives sorted order in BST)
Postorder: Left → Right → Root
Level-order: Level by level (uses queue)
Each traversal has specific applications

questions.question: Explain the Binary Search Tree (BST) data structure. Include operations like insertion, deletion, and search with their time complexities.
keyPoints: BST is a binary tree where left child < parent < right child
Enables efficient searching, insertion, deletion in O(h) time
Insertion: Compare with nodes, go left if smaller, right if larger
Deletion: Three cases - leaf node, one child, two children (find inorder successor)
Search: Compare target with current node, traverse accordingly
In balanced BST, height is O(log n), operations are O(log n)
Worst case (skewed tree) gives O(n) complexity

questions.question: Discuss the sorting algorithms: Bubble Sort, Merge Sort, and Quick Sort. Compare their time and space complexities.
keyPoints: Bubble Sort: O(n²) time, O(1) space, simple but inefficient, stable
Merge Sort: O(n log n) time, O(n) space, divide and conquer, stable
Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst, O(log n) space, unstable
Bubble sort compares adjacent elements and swaps
Merge sort divides array, sorts, and merges
Quick sort partitions around pivot element
Choice depends on data size, stability needs, space constraints

questions.question: Explain the Graph traversal techniques - BFS and DFS. Include their applications, data structures used, and time complexity.
keyPoints: BFS: Explores level by level, uses Queue data structure
DFS: Explores depth first, uses Stack or recursion
BFS Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V) for queue
DFS Time: O(V+E), Space: O(V) for recursion stack
BFS applications: Shortest path in unweighted graph, Level order traversal
DFS applications: Topological sorting, Cycle detection, Path finding
Both visit all vertices in connected graph

questions.question: What is Dynamic Programming? Explain with the Fibonacci series example and discuss its advantages over naive recursion.
keyPoints: DP solves problems by breaking into overlapping subproblems
Stores results (memoization/tabulation) to avoid recomputation
Fibonacci: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)
Naive recursion: Exponential time O(2^n), many repeated calculations
DP approach: O(n) time by storing previous two values
Eliminates redundant computations
Used in optimization problems like Knapsack, Longest Common Subsequence
